text: Here we present a video of robotic ampulectomy for a peri-ampullary neoplasm. Our patient is a fifty nine year old female who was evaluated for upper abdominal pain of four months duration which was associated with loss of appetite and non quantified weight loss. Ultrasound of abdomen showed a dilated CVD with soft tissue mass in the distal one-third. The blood investigations were normal.

MRCP showed a peri-emplary neoplasm with dilatation of both the bile and the pancreatic ducts. PET-CT scan showed a hypermetabolic soft tissue density in the peri-emplary region with ductal dilatation and no FDG-avid lymph nodes. While the above investigations were pointing towards malignant etiology, interestingly, biopsy showed tumor with low to high-grade dysplasia.

The patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg, and robotic ports are placed for the procedure. The surgeon begins by dissecting the gastrocolic ligament to mobilize the transverse colon inferiorly. Omental adhesions to the gallbladder and liver are freed, continuing laterally until the hepatic flexure is mobilized. The duodenum's lateral attachments are freed, and it is retracted above using prograsp forceps, carefully dissecting it away from the inferior vena cava.

The ampulla is localized and pulled out through a duodenotomy made on the lateral wall using an ultrasonic energy device and extended longitudinally with hook electrocautery. Stay sutures are placed to open the duodenal wall for retraction. Ampullary transduction begins in the inframedial aspect using ultrasonic energy, with visible pancreatic juice flowing from a dilated pancreatic duct. A stitch is placed between the pancreatic duct and duodenal mucosa to prevent retraction and aid traction. The dissection continues superiorly, with bile flow observed from the bile duct.
The surgeon displays a dilated bile duct and pancreatic duct. The specimen is placed in an endo bag and removed for frozen section biopsy.

The surgeon begins reconstruction of the common bile duct (CBD) by suturing the superior aspect of the CBD to the duodenal mucosa using PDS-C-C sutures. A bridging anastomosis is performed between the CBD and the pancreatic duct, followed by completing ductal reconstruction with interrupted sutures around the area.

During the robotic transduodenal ampullectomy, stay sutures are placed over the duodenum to assist in the closure of the duodenotomy using a blue EndoGIS stapler. The duodenotomy is closed transversely to prevent stricture formation. A drain is positioned in the right subhepatic space, and the omentum is placed over the duodenotomy site.

Patient was started on oral chips on post-op day two. The drain was removed on day five and patient was discharged on day seven. The final histopathology report was intra-ampullary papillary tubular neoplasm with low-grade dysplasia with negative margins. These are rare neoplasms of the ampulla of Werther. They are biologically indolent but can follow adenoma carcinoma sequence. Even though invasive lesions follow malignant course, they have better prognosis than typical ampullary carcinoma.
Thank you for watching the video.
